Now that the US is out, I'm rooting for Norway. 🇳🇴🇳🇴🇳🇴 

1) Haaland has won my heart. Partly because he is carnivore-ish (like me! 🥩🥩🥩), but also he seems so likable!
2) I fucking love Norway. Fell in love with it the first time I went to Oslo. 😍
3) The Norwegian row. Everyone loves this. 🚣🚣🚣
4) Their Viking photo. 🤯🤯🤯 There's no cooler team than this. This alone wins it for me. 💃💃💃
5) They brought a bunch of their own food to the US -- because they know (like me) that food has a direct impact on health and performance (and that a lot of the "food" in the US is poison). 🐟🧀🍊
6) I'm fascinated by Viking culture. They were brilliant (learned how to sail the open seas before compasses) and extremely spiritual, have incredible mythology and were the first Europeans to set foot in the New World. ⚔️⛵️🌊
7) The last time they were in a World Cup was 1998! I love rooting for an underdog.
Go Norway!!!

Hedge fund profits made during all manner of rebalances is directly sourced from the AUM of investors in index ETF's leveraged ETF's and hard benchmark index tracking mutual funds, insurance products, and pension funds 

Just in case you didn't realize where these returns come from.  

It's the "cost" paid by people who will not tolerate any slippage vs their benchmark.  The hard benchmark causes entirely inelastic buying and selling at a particular moment in time and pays massive bid offer spread to achieve the benchmark

tweets.text:
Some thoughts on Venice and revenue as it relates to VVV:

> Venice earns revenue through monthly subscription plans to the Venice platform (Pro $18/month, Pro Plus: $68/month & Max $200/month) plus additional revenue from API credits.

> Venice currently has around 3 million active users, 1.7M API calls per day and 200 models available. In terms of the discretionary VVV buyback a portion of the revenue from monthly subscriptions id directed back to VVV (Pro $2, Pro Plus, $5 & Max $10).

> This equates to 11.1%, 7.35% and 5% respectively for the revenue of the monthly subscription plans going back to VVV holders. Note there is no obligation for Venice to continue this buyback but I do not doubt they will not support VVV holders in some way with their revenue.

> Venice as reported is generating around $70M/year in revenue which translates to 3.5-7.8M for discretionary VVV buybacks. If VVV holders did have a legal claim on Venice revenue it would look cheap. But they do not and I would treat the above revenue as a base.

> VVV as an asset should most likely be valued on the revenue it receives from Venice for buybacks PLUS the native demand for DIEM as a compute asset in which users have a right for daily access to Venice compute.

Equity holders in Venice clearly have a much stronger claim than VVV holders for all things relevant to the growth of the Venice platform. I still think VVV as an asset is a very interesting experiment as it relates to compute access. But I think the Series A set in stone what VVV holders are eligible to as it relates to Venice ownership.

tweets.text:
Was a sad few months for $RDDT. But glad it's finally back above $200. 

Reddit was doing:
- $663M revenue w/ 91.5% gross margins
- $204M GAAP net income
- 45%+ fwd Y/Y growth after 69%+ growth.
- Net profit is ~30.7% of revenue

Felt very weird to see a profitable company get dragged down after earnings. 

But in hindsight, given the increase hyperscaler capex, the drop felt more like it's more relative opportunity cost more than fundamentals?

Since a lot of inflow poured into $MU / Sk Hynix that are bottlenecked into 2029. Or with laser bottlenecks like $LITE that last into 2029 as well. 

Same thing happens in reverse after selloffs ig, even if fundamentals didn't change. 

Been seeing a lot of noise with memory optimization or Chinese players... but don't quite think you sign 16T+ LTAs if memory was getting flooded or not used anytime soon. 

Same with optical players... if in earnings, they state anything they make gets bought, I don't quite think a lot of the noise has material effect on their fundamentals. 

Regardless, nice to see some recovery with familiar faces like $HOOD, $HIMS, and $RDDT though.

tweets.text:
1. I don't think they're in a dire need for financing. 

My take is half geopolitical, half valuation scarcity as the first pure play humanoid player. The latter is likely more valuable than private funding in my view for momentum. 

Unitree was going public in China and there's no pure play US humanoid exposure. SPAC structure is used for faster listing to give investors exposure, and they're both going public around the same timeframe. 

Otherwise a ton of Western capital inflow would have went over to China if there's no US humanoid exposure. 
I don't quite think it's coincidence in timing. 

2. I'm citing last private valuations for Figure. I personally don't believe it's worth $39B. 

But if it goes public, I do believe it would remain around that valuation in US markets, looking at what $SPCX did at $1.75T, just due to valuation anchoring psychology. 

Even so, investors are still paying 4x+ NAV to access Figure with CEFs and I'm amused by the logic behind that. 

I personally wouldn't assign it that value comparing it to Unitree at at $6B. (But I do think Unitree is undervaluing itself, probably ends up $12B+). But if you look at how $TSLA got their $1T+ valuation, a lot of it is anchored to the humanoid mission, so hard to value the sector. 

3. Same point as #1. Not exactly just to raise funds. 

Regardless, I'd encourage more companies to go public early on like $CCXI. Than waiting on when they already get big like Anthropic/OpenAI/SpaceX and letting retail be the last to invest.

tweets.text:
well, for one apps have no way to capture the base fee. the protocol can set a minimum fee to use the chain, and for all on-chain operations that fee cannot be circumvented. unlike prio fees, which are a function of conflict, and these conflicts can be upstreamed.

apps can and will create rollups or other mechanisms to move computation off-chain, and chains will compete with each other to push down the base fee. we have seen that dynamic play out for years.

so in general, the base fee per txn will of course compress. in my post, I was using $0.01/txn as an example, but it could be even lower. the difference is that there can be millions of txns happening at that price
